%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsubsection{Species occupancy data}
%%%%%%%%%%%%

Similar to mark-recapture data, species occurrence data is characterised by whether a species is present at a location and, conditional on its presence, whether it observed.
%by both species presence and the probability of observation (or re-capture).

%mixute model
N-mixture model?

As with CMR data, we can simulate this as two Bernoulli processes in a multi-response model; the probability of occurrence in a location and the probability of observation. We can simply multiply the two simulated response variables together to get the actual observation.

We can imagine that occurrence might be spatially autocorrelated (individuals are more likely to be found near others), if for example resources are spatially distribution.  

From existing temporal or spatial data, these correlation matrices can be generated using the \texttt{corClasses} functions in the \texttt{nlme} R package, and passed to the \texttt{cov\_str} argument of \texttt{simulate\_population()}.
